name: Zhao Yun
description: Zhao Yun, also known as Zhao Zilong, is a revered historical figure who has been heavily mythologized in Chinese culture, particularly within the context of the Three Kingdoms period (220–280 AD). While based on a real historical general, Zhao Yun's exploits have been embellished and transformed over centuries of storytelling, solidifying his place as a demigod-like figure in popular imagination. He is celebrated for his unparalleled loyalty, courage, and martial prowess, often depicted as the epitome of a virtuous warrior. Beyond his battlefield skills, Zhao Yun is admired for his unwavering dedication to his lord, Liu Bei, and his commitment to justice and righteousness. His character embodies the Confucian ideals of loyalty, filial piety, and benevolence. Through popular novels, operas, and folklore, Zhao Yun's legend has transcended historical accounts, establishing him as a symbol of heroism and moral integrity within Chinese cultural consciousness. He is often invoked as a protector and a symbol of unwavering strength.
subtitle: The Dragon General

keyMyths.title: Saving Liu Bei's Son, A Dou
keyMyths.description: One of Zhao Yun's most famous exploits involves rescuing Liu Bei's infant son, A Dou, during the Battle of Changban. Surrounded by enemy forces, Zhao Yun charged through the Cao Cao army single-handedly, retrieving the baby and delivering him safely to Liu Bei. This act cemented his reputation for unwavering loyalty and courage in the face of overwhelming odds. The myth highlights his protective nature and unparalleled martial skills.
keyMyths.source: Luo Guanzhong, Romance of the Three Kingdoms, Chapter 41

keyMyths.title: The Empty Fort Strategy
keyMyths.description: Although historically attributed to Zhuge Liang, some narratives associate Zhao Yun with the Empty Fort Strategy. During a critical moment, when vastly outnumbered, Zhao Yun is said to have calmly opened the city gates and played music, deceiving the enemy into believing there was an ambush. This act showcased not only his bravery but also his strategic thinking and ability to maintain composure under immense pressure, saving the city from certain defeat.
keyMyths.source: Pei Songzhi's Annotations to Records of the Three Kingdoms

keyMyths.title: Zhao Yun and the Jiang Xia Campaign
keyMyths.description: During the Jiang Xia Campaign, Zhao Yun displayed exceptional tactical acumen and leadership. He led a small force to successfully defend against a much larger enemy army, utilizing clever strategies and unwavering resolve. His ability to outmaneuver and defeat the enemy, despite being significantly outnumbered, further solidified his image as a brilliant military strategist and a formidable warrior capable of achieving victory against all odds. This myth highlights his strategic brilliance.
keyMyths.source: Chen Shou, Records of the Three Kingdoms, Biography of Zhao Yun

sources.author: Luo Guanzhong
sources.description: A 14th-century historical novel that heavily fictionalizes the events of the Three Kingdoms period, contributing significantly to the mythologization of Zhao Yun.
sources.text: A 14th-century historical novel that heavily fictionalizes the events of the Three Kingdoms period, contributing significantly to the mythologization of Zhao Yun.

sources.author: Chen Shou
sources.description: A 3rd-century historical text that provides the primary historical account of the Three Kingdoms period, upon which later legends were based.
sources.text: A 3rd-century historical text that provides the primary historical account of the Three Kingdoms period, upon which later legends were based.

sources.author: Rafe de Crespigny
sources.work: A Biographical Dictionary of Later Han to Three Kingdoms (23-220 AD)
sources.description: A comprehensive scholarly resource that provides detailed biographical information on historical figures from the Later Han and Three Kingdoms periods, including Zhao Yun.
